key_insight: A fully differentiable, GPU-accelerated FEM framework can simulate and invert complex finite-strain anisotropic plasticity models efficiently, enabling practical parameter identification from full-field data.
engineering_relevance: This work speeds up nonlinear mechanics simulation and makes gradient-based calibration of advanced constitutive models feasible for high-throughput materials design and manufacturing.
method: JAX-based FEM with end-to-end automatic differentiation
GPU parallelization of element assembly, sparse assembly, and sparse solves
Inverse characterization using topology-optimized specimens and displacement-field data
evidence_signal: On a 3 million-DOF forward problem, the method reached up to 9.4× speed-up over a 24-core CPU Abaqus baseline and accurately recovered anisotropic yield and hardening parameters across uniform and spatially varying cases.
watch_item: Performance and robustness depend on GPU memory, sparse solver efficiency, and the quality/identifiability of the measured full-field data.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
